s: What is Strzyga in mythology?
q: What mythological creature or entity is described?
e: female demon in Slavic mythology

s: What is Alraune in mythology?
q: Which German mythological creature is said to have human-like roots and magical properties?
e: German mythological creature

s: Benthesikyme is a sea goddess in Greek mythology, daughter of Poseidon and Amphitrite.
q: Who are Benthesikyme's parents in Greek mythology?
e: As the daughter of the sea god Poseidon and his queen Amphitrite, Benthesikyme was a goddess of the waves. She raised the young god Eumolpus after he was cast into the sea. Her name translates roughly to 'wave of the deep,' reflecting her domain over the ocean's movements.

s: Caligine is a Greek primordial divinity representing mist and darkness at the beginning of creation.
q: What does Caligine represent in Greek cosmogony?
e: In some Greek cosmogonic traditions, Caligine (also called Caligo) was the primordial mist or darkness that existed before the universe took form. Along with Chaos and Nyx (Night), Caligine was among the first abstract forces from which the cosmos emerged. This concept reflects ancient attempts to explain what existed before creation.

s: Clotho is one of the three Fates (Moirai) in Greek mythology, responsible for spinning the thread of life.
q: What is Clotho's role among the three Fates?
e: The three Fates worked together to control every mortal's destiny: Clotho spun the thread of life, Lachesis measured its length, and Atropos cut it at death. Clotho's spinning determined the moment of birth and the nature of each person's life. Even the gods respected and feared the Fates' decisions.

s: Dawon is a sacred tiger in Hindu mythology that serves as the mount of the goddess Durga.
q: Which Hindu goddess rides the sacred tiger Dawon?
e: In Hindu tradition, Dawon is a fierce and powerful tiger (sometimes depicted as a lion) gifted to the warrior goddess Durga by the gods. Durga rides Dawon into battle against demons, and the tiger symbolizes her unlimited power and the wild, untameable force of righteousness.

s: Death is a stock character in the danse macabre medieval tradition.
q: What medieval tradition features Death as a main character?
e: Death literally dances with the living in eerie medieval art—a chilling reminder of mortality!

s: Dibulok is a supernatural character from Swedish folklore.
q: Which country's folklore features the character Dibulok?
e: Swedish folklore is rich with supernatural beings that inhabit forests, mountains, and waterways. Dibulok belongs to the tradition of creatures that Scandinavian communities told stories about to explain natural phenomena and to warn people about dangerous places. These folklore characters reflect the deep connection between Nordic peoples and their natural environment.

s: Dokkakgwi (dokkaebi) are mischievous goblin-like creatures from Korean folklore.
q: What are dokkaebi in Korean folklore?
e: Dokkaebi are among the most beloved creatures in Korean mythology. Unlike Western goblins, they are often playful and can be tricked by clever humans. They carry magical clubs (bangmangi) that can summon objects, and they love wrestling competitions. They are said to be born from discarded household objects imbued with human spirit.

s: Ekecheiria is the Greek spirit personifying the Olympic truce, a sacred ceasefire during the ancient Games.
q: What does Ekecheiria personify in Greek mythology?
e: During the ancient Olympic Games, a sacred truce called the ekecheiria was declared across all Greek city-states. Wars were paused, armies stood down, and athletes could travel safely to Olympia. Ekecheiria was personified as a divine spirit ensuring peace during these athletic competitions.

s: Eleutherus is one of the Curetes in Greek mythology, divine warriors who protected the infant Zeus.
q: What did the Curetes do to protect infant Zeus?
a: Clashed shields to drown out his cries
e: The Curetes were a group of divine beings on Crete who clashed their shields and danced loudly to drown out the cries of baby Zeus, hiding him from his father Kronos who wanted to swallow him. Without their protection, Zeus would never have survived to overthrow the Titans.

s: Epiales is the spirit of nightmares in Greek mythology.
q: What does Epiales cause in Greek mythology?
e: Epiales was a demonic spirit who caused nightmares by sitting on sleepers' chests, creating a sensation of suffocation and terror. This concept parallels the incubus/succubus traditions and the Norse mara (from which 'nightmare' derives). The ancient Greeks took dreams very seriously, believing they could be messages from the gods or attacks by malevolent spirits.

s: Eris is the Greek goddess of discord and strife who triggered the Trojan War.
q: What did Eris throw to cause discord among the gods?
e: When Eris was not invited to the wedding of Peleus and Thetis, she threw a golden apple inscribed 'For the Fairest' among the goddesses, sparking a divine beauty contest between Hera, Athena, and Aphrodite. Paris of Troy judged Aphrodite the winner, and the resulting chain of events led to the Trojan War.

s: Eros is the Greek god of love and desire, one of the most ancient deities in Greek cosmogony.
q: In Hesiod's Theogony, Eros is described as what?
e: In Hesiod's Theogony, Eros was one of the primordial gods who emerged at the dawn of creation alongside Chaos and Gaia. Later traditions reimagined him as the son of Aphrodite, a mischievous youth with a bow. His Roman counterpart is Cupid, and his arrows could make anyone — god or mortal — fall helplessly in love.

s: A fravashi is a Zoroastrian concept of a personal guardian spirit that exists before birth and after death.
q: What is a fravashi in Zoroastrianism?
e: In Zoroastrianism, every person has a fravashi — a guardian spirit created before their physical body. These spirits chose to enter the material world to fight evil alongside Ahura Mazda. After death, the fravashi returns to the spiritual realm and continues to protect descendants and the living.

s: Harmonia is the ancient Greek goddess of harmony and concord, daughter of Ares and Aphrodite.
q: Who are Harmonia's parents in Greek mythology?
e: Born from the union of the god of war and the goddess of love, Harmonia represented the balance between conflict and affection. She married Cadmus, the founder of Thebes, and received a cursed necklace at her wedding that brought misfortune to all who owned it. Despite her name, her story is filled with tragedy.

s: Heimarmene is the Greek goddess personifying fate and the allotted destiny of all beings.
q: What concept does Heimarmene personify?
e: In Greek philosophy and mythology, Heimarmene represented the cosmic force of predetermined destiny. Unlike the individual Fates (Moirai) who spun, measured, and cut the thread of life, Heimarmene embodied the abstract concept of fate itself. Stoic philosophers particularly embraced her as representing the rational order of the universe.

s: Hera Alexandros is a mythological epithet meaning 'Hera the Defender' or 'Hera the Protector of Men.'
q: What does the epithet 'Alexandros' mean when applied to Hera?
e: In ancient Greece, gods and goddesses had many epithets reflecting their different roles. Hera Alexandros depicted the queen of the gods in her capacity as protector and defender. This aspect of Hera contrasted with her more commonly known role as the jealous wife of Zeus.

s: Hiisi is a term in Finnic mythologies referring to sacred groves and later to malevolent forest spirits.
q: What did 'hiisi' originally refer to in Finnic mythology?
e: Originally, hiisi referred to sacred natural sites — groves, hills, and rocks — where pre-Christian Finns performed rituals. After Christianization, the word's meaning shifted to denote evil spirits and demons that inhabited wild places. This transformation reflects how pagan sacred sites were demonized by the new religion.

s: Hundun is a legendary Chinese figure representing primordial chaos, existing before the creation of the universe.
q: What happened when Hundun was given facial features in Chinese mythology?
d: He split into yin and yang
e: In Chinese mythology, Hundun was a faceless being of primordial chaos from the Zhuangzi. When the emperors of the North and South seas tried to give Hundun the seven openings of the face (eyes, ears, nostrils, mouth) as thanks for his hospitality, he died — symbolizing how imposing order on chaos destroys its essential nature.

s: Hypate is one of the three original Muses in early Greek mythology, associated with the lowest musical string.
q: What is Hypate's connection to music in Greek mythology?
a: Named after the lowest lyre string
d: Named after the highest lyre string
e: Before the canonical nine Muses, some ancient traditions recognized only three: Hypate, Mese, and Nete, each named after a string on the lyre. Hypate corresponded to the lowest-pitched string. This earlier tradition reflected the deep connection between music and divine inspiration in Greek culture.

s: Jean de l'Ours (John of the Bear) is a legendary half-bear, half-human hero from French and Pyrenean folklore.
q: What is Jean de l'Ours in French folklore?
e: According to the legend, Jean de l'Ours was born to a woman who was captured by a bear in the Pyrenees mountains. He inherited superhuman strength from his bear father and used it to perform great feats. The tale has parallels across European folklore, from the Norse Beowulf to the Italian Juan dell'Orso.

s: Kallone is a figure referenced in Plato's Symposium, associated with beauty and ideal form.
q: In which of Plato's works is Kallone referenced?
e: In Plato's philosophical dialogues, beauty played a central role in understanding truth and the ideal Forms. Kallone is connected to the Greek concept of kallos (beauty), which Plato used to describe the highest form of aesthetic and moral perfection that the soul aspires to reach.

s: Knockers are small, subterranean creatures from Cornish and Welsh mining folklore who tap on tunnel walls.
q: What did miners believe Knocker sounds indicated?
a: Rich ore veins or danger warnings
e: Miners in Cornwall and Wales believed Knockers (or Tommyknockers) were small fairy-like beings who lived in mines. Their knocking sounds could lead miners to rich ore veins or warn of imminent cave-ins. Miners would leave food offerings for them and considered it bad luck to whistle underground, as it angered the Knockers.

s: A Leyak is a fearsome flying head with dangling entrails from Balinese mythology.
q: What does a Leyak look like when flying in Balinese mythology?
a: A detached head with dangling entrails
e: In Balinese folklore, a Leyak is a witch who can detach her head from her body at night, flying through the darkness with organs trailing below. Leyaks are associated with Rangda, the demon queen of Balinese mythology. They are believed to haunt cemeteries and prey on newborn children and pregnant women.

s: Lyssa is the Greek spirit of mad rage, frenzy, and rabies.
q: What modern disease name derives from the Greek spirit Lyssa?
e: In Greek mythology, Lyssa was the personification of uncontrollable fury. In Euripides' tragedy Heracles, Hera sent Lyssa to drive Heracles insane, causing him to murder his own wife and children. The word 'lyssa' is also the root of 'lyssavirus,' the scientific name for the rabies virus, reflecting the ancient association between madness and the disease.

s: Melinoe is an ancient Greek goddess associated with ghosts and propitiation of the dead.
q: What is Melinoe associated with in Greek mythology?
e: Described in the Orphic Hymns, Melinoe was said to wander the earth at night leading a train of ghosts, causing nightmares and madness in those who saw her. She was sometimes described as the daughter of Zeus and Persephone, born at the mouth of the river Cocytus in the underworld.

s: Morag is a legendary lake monster said to inhabit Loch Morar, the deepest freshwater loch in Scotland.
q: In which Scottish loch does Morag allegedly live?
e: Like the more famous Nessie of Loch Ness, Morag is a cryptid reportedly seen multiple times in Loch Morar. Sightings date back to 1887, and local legends associate the creature with death omens. Loch Morar reaches depths of over 300 meters, making it Scotland's deepest freshwater body — deep enough to hide almost anything.

s: The Muldjewangk is a fearsome water creature from Australian Aboriginal mythology that inhabits the Murray River.
q: Which river does the Muldjewangk inhabit in Aboriginal mythology?
e: In Ngarrindjeri Aboriginal tradition, the Muldjewangk is a terrifying water being that lives in the Murray River and its lakes. It was said to drag people underwater, and the stories served as warnings to respect waterways. Early European settlers also reported strange sightings in the Murray, adding to the legend.

s: The Neikea are the personifications of quarrels and grievances in Greek mythology.
q: Who is the mother of the Neikea in Greek mythology?
e: Children of the goddess Eris (Discord), the Neikea embodied the bitter arguments and disputes that tear communities apart. Hesiod listed them among the dark spirits born from Eris, alongside other destructive forces like Ponos (Toil) and Lethe (Forgetfulness). They represented the social damage caused by unresolved conflicts.

s: Pandemos is a divine epithet of Aphrodite meaning 'of all the people' or 'common to all.'
q: What does the epithet 'Pandemos' mean for Aphrodite?
a: Of all the people / common love
e: Aphrodite Pandemos represented the earthly, universal aspect of love shared by all people, as opposed to Aphrodite Urania who represented heavenly, spiritual love. The philosopher Plato distinguished these two forms in his Symposium. Temples to Aphrodite Pandemos were found throughout ancient Greece.

s: Penia is the Greek goddess and personification of poverty and need.
q: What does Penia personify in Greek mythology?
e: In Plato's Symposium, Penia (Poverty) plays a crucial role in the birth of Eros (Love). At a feast of the gods, she lay with Poros (Resource/Plenty) while he was drunk, and from their union Eros was born — explaining why love is always needy yet resourceful. This philosophical myth shaped Western ideas about desire.

s: Philomelus is an ancient Greek deity credited with inventing the wagon and the plough.
q: What did Philomelus invent according to Greek mythology?
e: Son of Demeter and the mortal Iasion, Philomelus was a humble farmer who invented the ox-drawn wagon to help with agriculture. His mother Demeter was so proud that she placed him among the stars as the constellation Boötes. His story celebrates the virtue of honest labor and agricultural innovation.

s: Phrike is the personification of horror and trembling fear in Greek mythology.
q: What does Phrike personify in Greek mythology?
e: In Greek drama and mythology, Phrike represented the visceral, physical sensation of horror — the shudder and trembling that seizes a person in the face of something truly terrifying. She appeared in Greek tragedies as a force accompanying violent or supernatural events. The English word 'fright' may be etymologically related.

s: Psyche is the mythical mortal woman who became the goddess of the soul after her trials to win back Eros/Cupid.
q: What did Psyche become after completing her trials?
e: In the famous tale by Apuleius, Psyche was so beautiful that Aphrodite became jealous and sent her son Eros to curse her. Instead, Eros fell in love with Psyche. After Psyche broke a taboo by looking at Eros, she had to complete impossible tasks set by Aphrodite before being reunited with her beloved and granted immortality.

s: The Snow Lion is a mythical creature in Tibetan culture symbolizing fearlessness, joy, and the snowy mountains.
q: What does the Snow Lion symbolize in Tibetan culture?
e: In Tibetan Buddhist culture, the Snow Lion is one of the Four Dignities alongside the dragon, tiger, and garuda. It represents unconditional cheerfulness and the earth element. The creature is depicted as a white lion with a turquoise mane and is a symbol of Tibet itself, appearing on the Tibetan flag.

s: Thesmophoros is an epithet of Demeter meaning 'bringer of law' or 'lawgiver.'
q: What does Demeter's epithet 'Thesmophoros' mean?
e: As Demeter Thesmophoros, the grain goddess was honored as the establisher of civilized law and agricultural order. The Thesmophoria festival, one of the most widespread Greek festivals, was celebrated exclusively by women over three days. It involved secret rites connected to fertility and the agricultural cycle.

s: Thrasos is the Greek spirit personifying reckless boldness and insolence.
q: What quality does Thrasos personify?
e: In Hesiod's and other Greek sources, Thrasos represented the dangerous quality of excessive boldness that leads to reckless behavior. The Greeks valued courage (andreia) but distinguished it sharply from Thrasos — bold recklessness that ignores consequences. He was considered a companion of Hybris (arrogance).

s: Trécouche is a legendary monster said to roam the rivers of the Southwestern Ardennes region.
q: Where does the monster Trécouche allegedly roam?
e: In the folklore of the Ardennes, a forested region spanning Belgium, Luxembourg, and France, Trécouche was a fearsome water monster lurking in rivers. Such regional water monsters served as cautionary tales to keep children away from dangerous riverbanks and deep waters. The Ardennes region has particularly rich monster folklore due to its dense forests and winding rivers.

s: A vetala is a creature from Hindu mythology that inhabits corpses and can possess the dead.
q: What does a vetala inhabit in Hindu mythology?
e: Vetalas are supernatural beings in Hindu folklore that occupy corpses hanging from trees in cremation grounds. They are featured prominently in the famous collection Baital Pachisi (25 Tales of the Vetala), where King Vikramaditya must capture a vetala that tells him riddles. Vetalas possess vast knowledge and can see past, present, and future.

s: The Vrykolakas is a vampire-like undead creature from Greek folklore.
q: What is a Vrykolakas in Greek folklore?
e: Unlike Western vampires, the Vrykolakas of Greek folklore was believed to be a restless corpse that could knock on doors and call out names. If someone answered, they would die. The creature was thought to arise from improper burial, excommunication, or living a sacrilegious life. Villages would exhume suspected Vrykolakas and perform rituals to stop them.

s: Zeus Soter is an epithet of Zeus meaning 'Zeus the Savior,' worshipped as a protector and deliverer.
q: What does the epithet 'Soter' mean when applied to Zeus?
e: The epithet Soter (Savior) was applied to Zeus in his role as a divine protector who saved cities from danger, armies from defeat, and individuals from peril. Libations to Zeus Soter were traditionally poured at the end of banquets as a final toast. His cult was especially important in Athens and other major Greek cities.

s: In Norse mythology, Odin sacrificed one of his eyes at Mimir's Well in exchange for cosmic wisdom and knowledge of the future.
q: What did Odin sacrifice at Mimir's Well to gain wisdom?
d: His ability to see the future
e: The Allfather's willingness to sacrifice part of himself for wisdom shows how deeply Norse culture valued knowledge — even the king of the gods had to pay a steep price for it.

s: The Egyptian god Thoth was credited with inventing writing, mathematics, and the calendar — he was depicted as an ibis-headed man.
q: Which Egyptian god was credited with inventing writing and mathematics?
e: Ancient Egyptians attributed nearly all intellectual achievements to Thoth, making him perhaps the most cerebral deity in any mythology — a god of knowledge itself.

s: Japanese kitsune (fox spirits) grow additional tails as they age, with the most powerful nine-tailed kitsune being over 1,000 years old.
q: How many tails does the most powerful kitsune fox spirit have?
e: The number of tails a kitsune has is a visual power ranking system — each tail represents centuries of accumulated wisdom and magical ability, maxing out at nine.

s: In Hindu mythology, the churning of the Ocean of Milk (Samudra Manthan) produced 14 divine treasures including the elixir of immortality, amrita.
q: What is the elixir of immortality produced during the churning of the Ocean of Milk called?
e: This cosmic tug-of-war between gods and demons is one of mythology's most epic creation events — both sides cooperated to churn an entire ocean using a mountain as the churning rod.

s: The Celtic festival of Samhain, when the veil between the living and dead was thinnest, is the direct ancestor of modern Halloween.
q: Which ancient Celtic festival is the direct ancestor of Halloween?
e: What we now celebrate with candy and costumes began as an ancient Celtic ritual marking the boundary between the worlds of the living and the dead at harvest's end.

s: Anansi the Spider is a West African trickster god who outsmarted every creature to become the owner of all stories in the world.
q: Which West African trickster god became the owner of all stories in the world?
e: Anansi stories spread from the Ashanti people of Ghana throughout the African diaspora — he's proof that cleverness beats strength, one of folklore's most enduring messages.

s: In Slavic folklore, Baba Yaga lives in a hut that stands on giant chicken legs and can walk through the forest on its own.
q: What unusual feature does Baba Yaga's hut have in Slavic folklore?
e: A witch whose house is alive and walks around on poultry legs is one of folklore's most wonderfully bizarre images — and she's both helper and villain depending on the tale.

s: Maui, the Polynesian demigod, lassoed the sun with a rope made from his sister's hair to slow it down so days would be longer.
q: What did the Polynesian demigod Maui do to the sun?
a: Lassoed it to slow it down
d: Sang to it to slow down
e: Maui literally wrestled the sun into submission because his mother's bark cloth couldn't dry fast enough — one of mythology's most practical reasons for a cosmic feat.
